1: 1-2 Chronicles was written to the post-exilic community (ca. 400-350 BCE) as a theological retelling of the monarchy. The Chronicler's perspective differs from Samuel-Kings: (1) he focuses almost exclusively on Judah and the Davidic line (the northern kingdom barely appears); (2) he omits many of David's failures (Bathsheba, Absalom) while including his worship and temple preparations; (3) he emphasizes the Levitical worship structure, the temple, and its proper celebration; (4) he ends on an upbeat note (Cyrus's decree, 2 Chronicles 36:22-23) rather than Kings' ambiguous ending (Jehoiachin's release). The Chronicler is writing a theology of hope for the restored community: YHWH's covenant with David is still in force; the temple worship is the proper center of life; the exile was judgment but not the end.
2: The Sethite line (vv1-3: Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared) runs from Genesis 5:1-20. The Chronicler strips away the ages and narratives, retaining only the names — the genealogy functions as a statement of continuity, not a biographical record. The line through Seth (rather than Cain) signals the covenant lineage: Seth was appointed as Abel’s replacement and from his son Enosh ‘people began to call on the name of YHWH’ (Genesis 4:26).
3: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech — the final pre-flood generation. Enoch is simply listed (v3); the Chronicler omits the narrative of his non-death (‘God took him,’ Genesis 5:24), which spawned extensive Second Temple Jewish speculation about Enoch as heavenly scribe and apocalyptic seer (1 Enoch, Jubilees). The Chronicler’s genealogy is resolutely this-worldly: the names matter, not the legends surrounding them.
4: Noah and his three sons (Shem, Ham, Japheth) are the pivot of the genealogy: everything before is pre-flood prehistory; everything after represents the post-flood nations. The Table of Nations (Gen 10 = 1 Chronicles 1:5-23) is one of the most geographically comprehensive texts in the ancient world — a systematic mapping of known peoples and their ethnic relationships, composed in the early first millennium BCE and reflecting trade and diplomatic knowledge of the era.
5: The sons of Japheth represent the northern and western peoples from the Israelite geographic horizon. Gomer = the Cimmerians (Assyrian Gimirrai), a people from the Black Sea region who swept through Anatolia in the 7th century BCE — attested in Sargon II’s records. Magog = possibly the Scythians (Assyrian Ishkuzai), a steppe people; Ezekiel 38-39 uses Magog as the archetypal northern threat. Madai = the Medes, an Iranian people who co-destroyed Assyria (612 BCE). Javan = the Greeks/Ionians (Greek Iawones, Hebrew yāwān). Tubal and Meshech = Iron-Age peoples of Anatolia (Tabalian and Mushki kingdoms in modern Turkey), trading with Tyre in slaves and bronze (Ezekiel 27:13).
6: Ashkenaz = likely the Scythians (Assyrian Ishkuzai; in later Jewish tradition, Ashkenaz designated Germanic peoples, which is why European Jews are called Ashkenazim). Riphath (= Diphath in some manuscripts) — difficult to identify precisely; possibly Paphlagonians or Phrygians. Togarmah = the Hittite vassal kingdom of Tegarama (modern Gurun in Turkey); Ezekiel 27:14 notes Togarmah traded horses and mules with Tyre; Ezekiel 38:6 includes Togarmah in the coalition against Gog.
7: Elishah = possibly Cyprus (Alashiya, attested in Egyptian and Ugaritic records as a major copper-exporting island; Ezekiel 27:7 notes linen from Elishah for Tyre). Tarshish = a distant western trading port, usually identified with Tartessus in Spain (or possibly Sardinia); Jonah flees there as the farthest point from Israel’s coast. Kittim = Cyprus/Aegean peoples; in later texts it shifts to mean Rome (Daniel 11:30; 1 Macc 1 Chronicles 1:1). Dodanim (= Rodanim in Genesis 10:4) = Rhodes (Rodos).
8: The sons of Ham represent African and Canaanite peoples. Cush = upper Nile Africa (classical Ethiopia/Nubia; modern Sudan); the Cushite kingdom of Kush (ancient Meroe) was a major power. Egypt = Miṣrayim (a dual form, possibly North + South Egypt). Put = Libya (North African coast west of Egypt; attested in Egyptian records as Puṭ). Canaan = the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the land — their listing under Ham establishes the theological basis for Israelite displacement: Canaanites are not in the Shemite/Abrahamic covenant line.
9: The sons of Cush include Seba (upper Nile region), Havilah (gold-bearing land, Genesis 2:11; possibly Arabia), Raamah, and the trade-pair Sheba and Dedan (South Arabian kingdoms, major incense-route traders; the Queen of Sheba came from this region, 2 Chronicles 9:1). The sons of Raamah (Sheba and Dedan) are listed separately, demonstrating the Table of Nations’ interest in tracing trading networks as much as ethnic groups.
10: Nimrod is the Table of Nations’ only narrative interruption: he became the ‘first mighty warrior (gibbôr) on earth,’ founder of Mesopotamian cities (Genesis 10:8-12 adds: Babel, Erech, Accad, Calneh in Shinar, then Nineveh, Calah, and others). In the ANE tradition, Mesopotamian kingship was the paradigm of earthly power — the list of cities reads like the Sumerian King List’s urban centers. Nimrod has been identified variously with Gilgamesh, Naram-Sin, Tukulti-Ninurta I, and others, but no certain identification holds. He represents the founding of the imperial Mesopotamian political tradition that would eventually exile Israel.
11: Egypt’s sons are regions or sub-groups of the Nile Valley: Ludim (possibly Libyan mercenaries in Egyptian service), Anamim, Lehabim (= Libyans?), Naphtuhim (possibly the Delta region or northern Egypt — npt = Delta in Egyptian). These are ethnic-geographic designations for peoples within Egypt’s cultural sphere rather than named nations with capital cities.
12: Pathrusim = Upper Egypt (Paṯrōs in Hebrew, from Egyptian p3 t3 rsy, ‘the southland’; Isaiah 11:11 and Jeremiah 44:1 locate the Jewish diaspora there). Casluhim — obscure; the Table of Nations identifies them as the ancestors of the Philistines. This is the most politically significant genealogical note in this section: the Philistines, Israel’s most persistent enemy in the period of the judges and early monarchy, originated from Egypt’s broader cultural sphere. Caphtorim = the people of Caphtor (= Crete or the Aegean; Deuteronomy 2:23 and Amos 9:7 also connect the Philistines to Caphtor).
13: Canaan’s firstborn is Sidon — the Phoenician city-state that would become one of Israel’s most important trading partners and neighbors. Heth = the Hittites; in the patriarchal narratives, the Hittites (Heth) are Canaanite residents of Hebron (Gen 23). These are distinct from the Anatolian Hittite Empire — probably related descendants or a southern branch. The Hittite Neo-Hittite city-states (Carchemish, Hamath) persisted into the 9th-8th centuries BCE as Aramaic-speaking states.
14: The Jebusites = pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem (Jebus); David captured the city from them (2 Samuel 5:6-7). Amorites = a broad category for Canaanite highland peoples; in cuneiform, Amurru (Westerners) was a general Mesopotamian term for the peoples west of the Euphrates. Girgashites = mentioned only in the canonical lists of Canaanite nations; no extra-biblical attestation.
15: Hivites = possibly related to the Hurrians (known as Horites in the OT); the Hivites of Shechem (Gen 34) and Gibeon (Josh 9) were Canaanite inhabitants who negotiated with the patriarchs and Israel. Arkites and Sinites — city-based groups along the Phoenician coast; Arqa (modern Tell Arqa in Lebanon) is attested in Egyptian topographical lists. Arvadites = Arvad (modern Ruad, an island city off the Syrian coast; Ezekiel 27:8,11 records Arvad’s maritime role).
16: Zemarites = Simyra, a Phoenician coastal city (attested in the Amarna letters as Sumur). Hamathites = the people of Hamath (modern Hama on the Orontes River in Syria), a major Aramaic kingdom in the early Iron Age; ‘the entrance of Hamath’ marked Israel’s northern boundary in ideal maps (Numbers 34:8; 1 Kings 8:65). These final Canaanite peoples complete the list of nations that Israelite memory associated with the pre-conquest population of the land.
17: The sons of Shem represent the Semitic peoples. Elam = the ancient civilization of southwestern Iran (Khuzestan), a rival to Mesopotamian powers and a major actor in the patriarchal period (Genesis 14:1: Chedorlaomer king of Elam). Asshur = Assyria (the Assyrian heartland around the upper Tigris). Arphaxad = ancestor of Abraham (vv18,24-27); the name may relate to the Chaldean/Chaldaean region. Aram = the Arameans, centered in Syria (Damascus); the Hebrew of the OT is closely related to Aramaic, and the patriarchs have Aramean family connections (Genesis 28:5; Deuteronomy 26:5: ‘a wandering Aramean was my father’).
18: The Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber chain is the trunk line leading to Abraham. Eber is the eponymous ancestor of the ʿiḇriyyîm (Hebrews; Genesis 10:21 calls Shem ‘the father of all the sons of Eber’). The line through Eber represents the covenant lineage narrowing: from all Semitic peoples through Eber to Abraham to Israel. The name ‘Hebrew’ connects the people to this genealogical ancestor.
19: Peleg = the line that leads to Abraham (v25); his name means ‘division’ (pālg in Ugaritic; Hebrew pālag, to divide). The text explains: ‘in his time the earth was divided.’ The traditional interpretation connects this to the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11:1-9): the division of languages scattered humanity. Some scholars propose a geographic division (tectonic, irrigation, or political). The Babel narrative (Gen 11) likely follows chronologically at the Peleg generation, which the Chronicler omits but assumes. Joktan = the other branch from Eber, leading to South Arabian peoples (vv20-23).
20: The sons of Joktan (vv20-23) represent South Arabian peoples — the tribal geography of the Arabian Peninsula as known from Israelite trade contacts. Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth (= Hadramawt, the major South Arabian kingdom in classical antiquity, modern Hadramout in Yemen), Jerah (possibly a lunar-cult center, yaraḥ = moon).
21: Hadoram, Uzal (= Sanaa, the capital of Yemen in classical Arabic sources?), Diklah (possibly the date-palm region of the Arabian Peninsula — diqla = date palm in Aramaic). These South Arabian identifications align with what is known of pre-Islamic Arabian tribal geography and are partially confirmed by South Arabian inscriptions.
22: Ebal, Abimael, Sheba (the most famous South Arabian kingdom, center of the incense trade; the Queen of Sheba who visited Solomon represents this wealthy polity). The Sheba here in the Joktanite list (South Arabian) is distinct from the son of Raamah in the Hamite line (v9) — the Table of Nations reflects the complexity of Arabian peoples’ origins, distributing ‘Sheba’ traditions across two lineages.
23: Ophir = the legendary gold source of Solomon’s fleet (1 Kings 9:28; 1 Chronicles 10:11); probable location in South Arabia or East Africa (Somalia/Punt); some scholars propose India. The exact identification is disputed. Havilah (also a Cushite son in v9) and Jobab complete the Joktanite list. The summary ‘all these were sons of Joktan’ closes the South Arabian section and redirects attention to the line that leads to Abraham (v24).
24: The Chronicler now compresses the full Shem-to-Abraham genealogy of Genesis 11:10-26 into a bare name-list (vv24-27), stripping away the ages that Gen 11 preserves. The ages (Shem lived 500 years after the flood; Arphaxad lived 403 years after Shelah was born; etc.) were of interest to the Gen 11 author for establishing the chronological framework; the Chronicler’s interest is the covenant lineage, not the chronology.
25: Eber, Peleg, Reu — the middle section of the Shem-to-Abraham chain. Peleg is already explained at v19 (earth divided); Reu is otherwise unknown. The Chronicler passes through these names at speed — the genealogical purpose is to establish the unbroken line from Shem (the blessed son of Noah, Genesis 9:26) to Abraham without any gap.
26: Serug, Nahor, Terah — the final three links before Abraham. Nahor is Abraham’s grandfather (and also the name of Abraham’s brother, Genesis 11:26-29). Terah = Abraham’s father, who began the journey from Ur of the Chaldeans toward Canaan but died in Haran (Genesis 11:31-32). The city of Haran (modern Harran in southeastern Turkey) is archaeologically attested as a major Mesopotamian city and a center of moon-god worship.
27: Abram, that is, Abraham — the Chronicler uses both names without explanation: ʾaḇrām hûʾ ʾaḇrāhām. The parenthetical gloss (‘that is, Abraham’) acknowledges the name-change in Genesis 17:5 without retelling the covenant-renewal narrative behind it. The arrival at Abraham ends the universal history section (vv1-27) and begins the particular covenant history — the narrowing from all humanity to the one man through whom ‘all nations would be blessed’ (Genesis 12:3).
28: Abraham’s two covenantal sons are listed together: Isaac (the son of promise through Sarah) and Ishmael (the son of Hagar). The Chronicler places them on equal formal footing in the genealogical list before separating their lines (vv29-33 = Ishmael; v34 = Isaac). The equal listing in v28 reflects the genealogical fact — both are Abraham’s sons — without resolving the theological distinction that the narrative tradition maintains.
29: The Ishmaelite genealogy (vv29-31) traces twelve tribes, paralleling the twelve tribes of Israel — a structural deliberateness that signals Ishmael’s status as a ‘great nation’ promised to him (Genesis 17:20). Nebaioth = oldest brother; traditionally identified with the Nabataeans, the kingdom that controlled the incense routes from Arabia to the Mediterranean (capital: Petra; flourished 4th century BCE – 2nd century CE). Kedar = major North Arabian tribe, prominent in prophetic texts as desert-dwellers (Isaiah 60:7; Jeremiah 2:10; Ezekiel 27:21).
30: Mishma, Dumah, Massa — Dumah is identified with an oasis in northern Arabia (mentioned in Isaiah 21:11; Assyrian records: Adumatu); Massa appears in Proverbs 30:1 and 31:1 as the homeland of wisdom writers Agur and Lemuel, suggesting a North Arabian wisdom tradition. Hadad is a name borne by Edomite kings as well (v51), reflecting shared divine names across the Aramean-Arabian cultural sphere (Hadad = the storm god).
31: Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah complete the twelve Ishmaelite tribes. Jetur and Naphish are the tribes against whom the Transjordanian Israelites fought (1 Chronicles 5:19), associating these Ishmaelites with the Syrian desert fringe. The summary ‘These were the sons of Ishmael’ closes the Ishmaelite section; the genealogy then shifts to the Keturah-line (v32), the ‘lesser’ Abrahamic descendants through his concubine.
32: The sons of Keturah, Abraham’s concubine (Genesis 25:1-4): six sons representing Arabian and Midianite tribes. Zimran and Jokshan are otherwise unknown. Medan = possibly near Midian. Midian = the most significant of the six — the Midianites are central to the Moses and Exodus narratives (Jethro, Moses’s father-in-law, is a Midianite priest); they also appear as caravan traders who carried Joseph to Egypt (Genesis 37:28) and as Israel’s opponents in Judges (Gideon, Judg 6-8). Ishbak and Shuah (Shuah = Job’s friend Bildad the Shuhite, Job 2:11 — placing Job’s dialogue partners in this Arabian-adjacent cultural zone).
33: The sons of Midian include Ephah (mentioned in Isaiah 60:6 as bringing camels and gold from Midian to Jerusalem at the eschatological ingathering) and Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah. These represent the tribal sub-groups of Midian in the Transjordanian and North Arabian steppe. The summary closes the Keturah section; Abraham’s other descendants (through Hagar and Keturah) are located in the Arabian-steppe zone — the surrounding nations against whose backdrop Israel’s covenant identity is defined.
34: The pivot: ‘Abraham fathered Isaac. The sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel.’ The parallelism is exact — first Abraham and his two sons, now Isaac and his two sons. Esau = Edom (the name-equation is stated in Genesis 36:1,8). Israel = Jacob, whose name-change (Genesis 32:28; 1 Chronicles 35:10) the Chronicler again notes through the covenant name only, without the narrative. The selection of ‘Israel’ rather than ‘Jacob’ signals the Chronicler’s focus: what matters is the covenant identity, not the biographical individual.
35: The sons of Esau (vv35-37) parallel the structure of the Ishmaelite and Keturahite lists. Esau’s sons through his Canaanite and Horite wives: Eliphaz (the most significant — through him Amalek; and Eliphaz is the name of Job’s most prominent friend, Job 2:11, from Teman, an Edomite region). Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, Korah. The Edomite genealogy prepares for the king list in vv43-54.
36: The sons of Eliphaz include Teman — the most famous Edomite clan/region, renowned for wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7: ‘Is there no wisdom in Teman? Has counsel perished from the prudent?’; Obadiah 1:8-9). Amalek = the Amalekites; his mother is Timna (v36; Genesis 36:12: Timna was Eliphaz’s concubine), connecting Amalek to both the Edomite and Horite lines. The Amalekites are Israel’s first attacker in the wilderness (Exodus 17:8-16) and the object of YHWH’s perpetual war (Exodus 17:16).
37: The sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah — these four names reappear as Edomite chiefs in vv37 and 51-54 (Nahath = chief Aliah?), reflecting the clan-based leadership structure of Edom. The Edomite social structure was organized around clans (ʾallûpîm, chiefs) rather than tribes in the Israelite sense.
38: The sons of Seir (vv38-42) — the Horites, the pre-Edomite inhabitants of the land of Edom (the region south of the Dead Sea). Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. The Horites (traditionally identified with the Hurrians, a people of northern Mesopotamian origin who settled widely in the 2nd millennium BCE) were the indigenous population before Esau’s descendants displaced and intermarried with them (Genesis 36:20-30). The genealogical inclusion of both Edomite and Horite lines reflects the historical intermingling of these populations.
39: The sons of Lotan: Hori and Homam. Timna was Lotan’s sister — the same Timna who became the concubine of Eliphaz (v36), uniting the Edomite and Horite lines through her. This cross-reference is the genealogy’s subtlest note on Edomite-Horite integration: the concubine who bore Amalek is herself a Horite woman, making Amalek genetically Horite as well as Edomite.
40: Shobal’s sons include Alian, Manahath, Ebal — Manahath is also the name of a Benjaminite place of exile (1 Chronicles 8:6) and a clan within Judah (1 Chronicles 2:52,54), suggesting that Edomite/Horite names infiltrated the tribal territories through later settlement patterns. Zibeon’s sons: Aiah and Anah — the same Anah who discovered hot springs in the wilderness (Genesis 36:24; LXX has ‘water’, MT has ‘mules’/‘springs’), a detail about Anah’s character that the genealogy preserves.
41: Anah’s son Dishon (distinguishable from the Dishon of v38, Seir’s son). Dishon’s sons: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran — four Horite clan-names otherwise unattested outside the genealogical tradition. These represent the sub-tribal structure of the Horite/Seir population.
42: The sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, Jaakan (yaʿăqān — same root as Jacob?). Bilhan is also a Benjaminite name (1 Chronicles 7:10). The sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran. Uz = the land of Job (Job 1:1); the geographic association of Uz with the Edomite/Horite region places the Job narrative in the south Transjordanian zone, consistent with the book’s setting among non-Israelite sages with Edomite connections (Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite).
43: The Edomite king list (vv43-54 = Genesis 36:31-43) is introduced with a historically significant phrase: ‘these are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel.’ The Edomites had a monarchy before Israel — the list predates Saul (ca. 1020 BCE). The kings in the list are non-dynastic: each comes from a different clan, with no succession from father to son. Bela son of Beor (whose city was Dinhabah) — the only element of this list that has attracted modern comparison is ‘son of Beor,’ which is also Balaam’s patronymic (Numbers 22:5); the connection is almost certainly coincidental.
44: Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah — Bozrah (modern Buseirah, Jordan) was one of the most important Edomite cities, the likely administrative center of the Edomite kingdom in the Iron Age. Excavations at Buseirah have revealed a major Iron Age fortress and administrative complex (ca. 8th-7th century BCE). Bozrah is mentioned prominently in prophetic judgments against Edom (Isaiah 34:6; 1 Chronicles 63:1; Jeremiah 49:13,22; Amos 1:12), indicating its status as Edom’s chief city.
45: Husham of the land of the Temanites — Teman was the most famous Edomite district, renowned for wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7; Obadiah 1:8). Husham’s origin in Teman places a third of the early Edomite kings in the southern region of Edom (around the Wadi Arabah). The land of Teman = the northern Edomite plateau; Temanite wisdom is attested through Eliphaz the Temanite (Job 2:11) as the preeminent friend of Job.
46: Hadad son of Bedad, who ‘defeated Midian on the territory of Moab’ — this is the only king in the list with a recorded military action. The battle against Midian in Moabite territory is unattested elsewhere but fits a period of Midianite-Moabite conflict in the region. Hadad is a storm-god name widespread in the Aramean-Edomite cultural zone (the Aramean god Hadad = Baal; Edomite naming the king Hadad). His city was Avith.
47: Samlah of Masrekah — location uncertain (perhaps in northern Edom); no recorded action. The succession formula is consistent: each king succeeds the previous one without relationship (wayyimlōk taḥtāyw, ‘and he reigned in his place’). This non-dynastic succession pattern distinguishes Edom from the later Davidic dynasty, where hereditary succession was the Chronicler’s ideal.
48: Shaul of Rehoboth-by-the-River — ‘the River’ most likely = the Euphrates (as in most OT usage), which would make Rehoboth-by-the-River a city in northern Syria; alternatively, the Wadi Zered (a valley in Moab/Edom) has been proposed. This king may represent a period of Edomite territorial expansion northward, or the name may reflect a different river entirely. The Shaul/Saul correspondence with Israel’s first king is noted by some commentators but may be coincidental.
49: Baal-hanan son of Achbor — the only compound Baal-name in the Edomite king list, indicating that Baal worship was present in the royal naming convention. Achbor is also the name of Josiah’s secretary (2 Kings 22:12,14), suggesting the name was current in later periods as well. Baal-hanan’s reign follows the standard succession formula without military note.
50: Hadad — a second king named Hadad (as in v46; the duplication reflects common Edomite royal naming). His city was Pai (= Pau in Genesis 36:39). His wife was Mehetabel daughter of Matred daughter of Me-zahab — the only queen mentioned in the list, and uniquely with a two-generation matrilineal genealogy. This detail is preserved from the original Edomite court records and reflects the importance of the queen mother’s lineage in the Near Eastern court tradition (cf. the gᵉḇîrāh institution in Judah).
51: ‘Hadad also died’ — this phrase (the last king’s death without a named successor) suggests the king list was compiled at a moment when dynastic succession had broken down or the list had reached its source’s end. The transition from kings to chiefs (ʾallûpîm) (vv51-54) may reflect a later period when Edom was organized tribally rather than monarchically, or a different source tradition. The chiefs may have been contemporaneous with the kings rather than successive.
52: Chiefs Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon — Oholibamah is also the name of one of Esau’s wives (Genesis 36:2,5,14), a Horite woman; the chief’s name matching a wife’s name suggests clan naming after eponymous ancestors. Pinon = possibly Feinan (ancient Punon), a major copper-smelting center in the Arabah valley south of the Dead Sea, attested in Egyptian records as a source of copper and location of Israelite stops in the wilderness (Numbers 33:42-43: Punon).
53: Chiefs Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar — Kenaz reappears as a clan within Judah: Caleb’s father was Jephunneh the Kenizzite (Numbers 32:12); Othniel son of Kenaz was the first judge of Israel (Judges 3:9). The incorporation of a Kenizzite (Edomite-adjacent) clan into Judah is a historical example of the ethnic fluidity at the margins of Israelite identity. Teman appears again as a chief, confirming the region’s importance.
54: Chiefs Magdiel and Iram complete the list. The closing formula ‘These were the chiefs of Edom’ ends chapter 1. The Chronicler’s decision to include the Edomite genealogy and king list in full — despite the Edomite-Israelite enmity — reflects the genealogical comprehensiveness of the Table of Nations framework: all nations are traced to their origins, establishing YHWH’s sovereignty over all peoples, not only Israel. The Edomite list also implicitly sets up the subsequent Davidic monarchy as the fulfillment of Israel’s own delayed kingship — the nations had kings first, but Israel’s king would ultimately be greater.
